# Spacetime Lessons and Open Boundaries

Part III began with a simple claim: gravity is the first place where structured spacetime becomes unavoidable in M1. The chapters in between have now shown what that claim amounts to at the present maturity level.

The result is neither trivial nor final. Gravity has already forced the framework to adopt a real source, field, and observer grammar, to distinguish scalar well from directional shift structure, to read clocking and transport against a structured background, and to admit a bounded nonstationary extension track above the stationary backbone. But those gains do not yet amount to a full derivation of spacetime from deeper first principles. They amount to a disciplined structural lesson.

## What gravity has already forced M1 to say

The first lesson is that spacetime cannot remain a merely passive backdrop once gravity becomes part of the story. The stage acquires real structure. Sources do not act directly on distant targets in one step. They feed a field layer, and the field layer then changes local clocking, transport, and observer readout.

The second lesson is that this structure is not exhausted by a single scalar well. The gravity program already requires at least a scalar and a directional shift distinction. The even source channel and the odd source channel do different work, and the field they generate preserves that distinction.

The third lesson is that observer bookkeeping matters. Gravity is not fully characterized by source equations or field equations alone. The framework must also say how local magnitudes and local clocks are read once the background is structured.

## What has been earned most strongly

The most strongly earned result is the stationary backbone.

The source-side parity packaging is clear. The scalar-well plus shift field grammar is clear. The stationary Newtonian limit is clear. The weak and moderate stationary dictionary is clear. The stationary Kerr pullback is strong enough to function as an exact target of the present overlap package. And the local moving-clock reading now has a disciplined gravity-side and motion-side split.

Those are not decorative achievements. They are the reason the gravity part can be written as a real engine chapter cluster rather than as a speculative preview.

## What the nonstationary extension adds

The nonstationary program adds something important, but narrower.

It adds a locked retarded scalar and shift baseline, exact stationary recovery, real field-work bookkeeping, and a structured hierarchy of promoted nonlinear and tensor layers. That is enough to say that time-dependent gravity is now part of the live framework. It is not enough to say that the entire gravity theory is finished.

The distinction matters because it protects the part from overclaiming. Stationary gravity is the achieved backbone. Nonstationary gravity is the bounded extension track growing out of that backbone.

## Correspondence, derivation, and open completion work

Part III has repeatedly used correspondence language, and that is appropriate. Some of its strongest results are best described as overlap or pullback results. The stationary Kerr package is the clearest example. It is an exact stationary target in M1 variables, but the full native rotating-source derivation remains open.

That pattern should be stated plainly. A successful correspondence result is not nothing, but it is also not automatically a full derivation. Part III therefore earns the right to speak confidently about some stationary structures while still keeping visible where the derivational burden remains.

The main unfinished boundaries are now also clear enough to name. Exact native rotating-source closure remains open. Full nonlinear closure remains open. The tensor and radiative sectors remain first-pass rather than final. And the deeper ontological question of what spacetime ultimately is in M1 remains downstream of the gravity results rather than fully settled by them.

## The honest closing posture

The right closing posture is therefore disciplined rather than triumphant.

Gravity has done enough to force structured spacetime into the framework as a real physical layer. It has not yet licensed the claim that every aspect of that structure is fully derived, fully closed, or fully interpreted. What Part III achieves is better than a slogan and less than a finished doctrine. It gives the reader a gravity package that can be followed, judged, and tested, together with a clear view of where the package still stops.

That is the right place to end the part. Gravity has shown that the inertial shell is not the whole story. The next task is to ask what additional internal structure is needed once one moves beyond gravity into the geometry of expression, state organization, and the quantum bridge that Part IV will take up.
